tier_1_organizational_context_modifiers.description: Questions establishing organizational context and constraints. These are always asked first to frame the negotiation boundaries for all subsequent maturity discovery. Answers steer dimension targets, compliance floors, and trade-off weighting across all 12 capabilities.

questions.question: How would you describe your organization's business volatility (e.g., frequency of strategic or operational changes)?
questions.purpose: Estimates the stability of processes and how often maturity practices may need adaptation. High volatility favours adaptive, lightweight practices over rigid organizational mandates.
questions.refines: Management dimension stability; weighting on proactive vs. predictive approach targets
questions.next_step_logic: High volatility lowers the ceiling on Management (Optimized may be unrealistic) and increases weight on Approach (Proactive becomes more valuable than Predictive). Low volatility enables full Predictive/Optimized targets.

questions.question: How critical is cybersecurity for achieving your core business goals?
questions.purpose: Determines how preventive or proactive the organization should aim to be. Directly sets the ambition level for the Main Approach dimension.
questions.refines: Upper maturity target for the Approach dimension across all capabilities
questions.next_step_logic: High criticality sets the Approach ceiling at Predictive (Level 4). Medium targets Proactive (Level 3). Low allows Reactive (Level 2) as an acceptable operational state.

questions.question: What is your organization's risk appetite in the context of cybersecurity investments?
questions.purpose: Indicates tolerance for risk and adjusts the trade-off between investment cost and maturity target during the negotiation phase.
questions.refines: Adjusts negotiation weighting in maturity optimization; influences capability selection breadth
questions.next_step_logic: Risk-Averse pushes all capabilities toward higher levels and broadens the set of mandatory capabilities. Risk-Tolerant allows selective investment in fewer capabilities at higher levels. Balanced uses the default dependency-driven selection.

questions.question: Which regulatory frameworks apply to your operations?
questions.purpose: Defines the minimum mandatory maturity level in relevant domains. Regulatory compliance establishes a non-negotiable floor.
questions.refines: Establishes compliance floor; activates mandatory practices in Governance (PRA-CS-GOV-08), Requirements (PRA-CS-REQ-01), and Risk Management (PRA-CS-RISK-01)
questions.next_step_logic: Each selected framework maps to a minimum maturity level for specific practices. NIS2 requires at least Level 3 in Governance, Risk Management, and Operational Security. ISO 27001 requires at least Level 3 across all capabilities. If none selected, compliance floor defaults to Level 0 (no constraint).

questions.question: How would you characterize your Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC) definition and formality?
questions.purpose: Evaluates process maturity baseline, impacting the Execution Scope dimension and setting feasibility constraints for practices that require organizational-level enforcement.
questions.refines: Sets dependency constraints for SDLC-integrated practices; bounds Execution Scope dimension
questions.next_step_logic: Undefined caps Scope at Individual for all practices. Basic allows Team-level scope. Formal enables Organizational scope. Integrated enables Predictive approach for SDLC-embedded practices (Security Testing, CI, CD).

questions.question: How many development teams does your organization have, and how autonomous are they in defining their own processes?
questions.purpose: Determines whether organizational-level practices are feasible or whether the model should emphasize team-level maturity. Directly influences the Scope dimension targets.
options: Single team or very small organization
Multiple teams with some central coordination
questions.refines: Scope dimension; feasibility of organizational-level practices (Level 3-4)
questions.next_step_logic: Single team makes organizational scope equivalent to team scope — Level 3 practices are achievable but framed as team practices. High autonomy teams cap many practices at Level 2 unless a coordination mechanism exists. Centralized governance enables full Level 3-4 targets.

questions.question: Does your organization rely on external providers for software development, infrastructure, or security services?
questions.purpose: Determines relevance and priority of supply-chain security practices. Directly activates or deprioritizes practices in Risk Management (PRA-CS-RISK-03), Configuration Management (PRA-CS-CFGM-02), and CI (PRA-CS-CI-03).
questions.refines: Activates PRA-CS-RISK-03, PRA-CS-CFGM-02, PRA-CS-CI-03; adjusts their maturity targets proportionally
questions.next_step_logic: No external providers marks PRA-CS-RISK-03 as not applicable and deprioritizes PRA-CS-CFGM-02. Significant/primary reliance elevates these to mandatory with Level 3 minimum target.

questions.question: Does your organization process personal data (PII) as part of its products or services?
questions.purpose: Determines the relevance and priority of PII-related practices in Governance.
questions.refines: Activates PRA-CS-GOV-07 (PII Inventory); adjusts GDPR-related compliance floor
questions.next_step_logic: No marks PRA-CS-GOV-07 as not applicable. Limited scope sets a minimum Level 2 target. Extensive processing sets a minimum Level 3 target and triggers a follow-up question on data protection officer existence.

questions.question: What is the primary deployment model for your software products?
questions.purpose: Determines the relevance and maturity targets for Operational Security practices, particularly infrastructure adaptation and deployment configuration.
questions.refines: Sets context for PRA-CS-OPSEC-01, PRA-CS-OPSEC-03, PRA-CS-OPSEC-04
questions.next_step_logic: On-premise caps PRA-CS-OPSEC-03 at Level 0. Cloud-native/SaaS elevates PRA-CS-OPSEC-03 and PRA-CS-OPSEC-04 to minimum Level 3 targets. Hybrid requires Level 2 minimum across operational security.

questions.question: How would you rate the overall cybersecurity knowledge and experience of your development and operations teams?
questions.purpose: Sets the baseline for the Knowledge dimension across all capabilities and determines the urgency of Governance training practices.
options: Basic awareness but limited hands-on experience
Deep expertise with specialized security roles
questions.refines: Knowledge dimension baseline; priority of PRA-CS-GOV-02 (training); feasibility of Level 3-4 targets
questions.next_step_logic: Minimal awareness caps Knowledge at Aware (Level 1) and elevates PRA-CS-GOV-02 to high priority. Proficient enables Level 3 targets. Deep expertise enables Level 4 and reduces priority of training practices.

tier_2_core_maturity_discovery.description: Adaptive questions aligned with the four maturity dimensions (Approach, Execution Scope, Knowledge, Management) and explicitly linked to specific capabilities and practices. The SLM selects questions dynamically based on Tier 1 answers and prior Tier 2 responses, optimizing for maximum information gain regarding uncertain maturity dimensions or dependencies.

questions.question: Does your organization have a documented security policy or a set of security-related working agreements?
questions.purpose: Determines whether a foundational security policy exists and at what level of formalization.

questions.question: Is the security policy or process continuously monitored and adjusted based on data or prediction models?
questions.purpose: Distinguishes Level 3 (organizational enforcement) from Level 4 (predictive adjustment).
questions.conditional: Only asked if CMD-GOV-01 >= 'Organization-wide enforced policy'

questions.question: How does your organization approach security awareness and training?
questions.purpose: Assesses the maturity of security knowledge development practices.
options: Ad-hoc Security Awareness Training identified by teams
Planned SAT with team-specific training needs

questions.question: Is there a defined security strategy that integrates security practices into the SDLC?
questions.purpose: Determines whether security is strategically embedded in the development lifecycle.
options: Team-level goals and practices defined for SDLC
Organization defines mandatory/optional SDLC security practices
Organization analyzes and adjusts SDLC practices based on outcomes

questions.question: Does your organization have dedicated security roles (e.g., security champions, a Security Center of Competence)?
questions.purpose: Assesses the existence and maturity of security-focused organizational profiles.
options: Organizational Security CoC with team satellites
CoC and satellites actively driving improvements

questions.question: Does your organization define and collect security-focused metrics?
questions.purpose: Determines whether security performance is measured and how metrics feed back into the process.
options: Organizational metrics catalog with per-team analysis
Org-level analysis with SDLC feedback integration

questions.question: How does your organization handle compliance obligations?
questions.purpose: Determines the maturity of compliance monitoring from reactive to predictive.
options: Organizational compliance catalog with SDLC translation
Monitored, enforced, and new regulations impact-assessed

questions.question: Does your organization evaluate applications and data from a security risk perspective?
questions.purpose: Determines whether risk management exists and at what scope it operates.
options: Risks identified but not properly handled
Organizational risk strategy with application classification
Organizational risk management with periodic SDLC-integrated analysis

questions.question: Are security risks used as a formal decision point for releasing software?
questions.purpose: Assesses whether risk analysis gates the release process.
options: Some risks considered, no explicit criteria
Team considers risks, no formal write-off
Release risk analysis drives SDLC improvement

questions.question: Does your organization systematically identify potential attackers, attack patterns, and vulnerabilities?
questions.purpose: Determines the maturity of threat intelligence and vulnerability management.
options: Per-stack probability analysis integrated into SDLC

questions.question: Does your organization produce threat assessments or abuse cases for its projects?
questions.purpose: Assesses the maturity of systematic threat evaluation.
options: Per-project threat assessment and abuse cases
Evaluation leads to SDLC control gate adjustments

questions.question: How does your organization manage regulatory requirements for its software products?
questions.purpose: Determines how regulatory needs are translated into actionable requirements.
options: Teams identify and translate per-product regulatory needs
Periodic review with impact analysis across products

questions.question: Does your organization maintain a defined inventory of security features (e.g., authentication, authorization, encryption) with consistent technology choices?
questions.purpose: Determines whether security features are managed as reusable organizational assets.
options: Some teams align on technology for some features
Organizational inventory with technology alternatives and objectives
Periodic analysis and SDLC promotion of feature usage

questions.question: Does your organization define and enforce the use of security-by-design components and services?
questions.purpose: Determines whether security-by-design is an explicit architectural concern with shared components.
options: Periodic analysis with predictive behavior modeling

questions.question: Does your organization use Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tools to manage external component usage?
questions.purpose: Assesses the maturity of dependency and supply-chain security analysis.
options: External dependencies managed, no integrity check
Team manages dependencies, no vulnerability analysis
Organizational SCA rules and enforced tool usage
SCA results used for predictive planning

questions.question: Does your organization have a defined security testing strategy?
questions.purpose: Determines the existence and scope of security verification practices.
options: Automated mandatory security testing with analysis

questions.question: What types of security tests are integrated into your development pipeline?
questions.purpose: Assesses the sophistication and automation level of security test suites.

questions.question: Does your organization perform penetration testing, and if so, does it include external testers?
questions.purpose: Determines the existence and scope of penetration testing practices.

questions.question: How is your deployment environment secured?
questions.purpose: Determines the maturity of deployment configuration security.

questions.question: What level of monitoring do you have over your deployed applications and infrastructure?
questions.purpose: Assesses the depth and sophistication of operational monitoring.

questions.question: How does your organization register and manage security incidents detected in operational environments?
questions.purpose: Determines the maturity of incident registration, triage, and response.
options: Incident exchange with response team, bugs created
Security incidents process periodically analyzed and improved

questions.question: Does your organization have a dedicated security event response team?
questions.purpose: Assesses the existence and training level of a response team.
options: Ad-hoc response by available team members
Organizational response team with mandatory training
Team periodically analyzes own performance for improvement

questions.question: Does your organization maintain a software asset inventory?
questions.purpose: Determines the existence and scope of asset tracking, which is a prerequisite for integrity control and SBOM.

questions.question: Does your organization produce a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)?
questions.purpose: Assesses SBOM generation capability and its integration into organizational processes.
options: Organizational SBOM definition, majority of products covered
SBOM processes periodically evaluated with SDLC feedback

questions.question: How does your organization manage source code repositories?
questions.purpose: Determines the maturity of version control and branching strategy.
options: Periodic consistency analysis and process evolution

questions.question: Does your organization have continuous integration capability?
questions.purpose: Assesses the existence and automation level of CI.
options: Organizational CI with quality-metric control gates
CI periodically evaluated for business impact

questions.question: Does your organization use static analysis tools?
questions.purpose: Determines the maturity of SAST tool integration.

questions.question: Does your organization have a global testing strategy with defined goals and metrics?
questions.purpose: Assesses the overall testing strategy maturity.
options: Team strategy with goals and manually collected metrics
Organizational strategy with quality metrics enforced
Strategy periodically reviewed for SDLC improvement

questions.question: Does your organization have a process to restore a product to a known stable state when a failure occurs?
questions.purpose: Determines the resilience and recovery capability.
options: Manual restore by specific people, probable data loss
Manual restore, no specific team member needed
Automated restore with data loss prevention
Automated restore periodically reviewed with SDLC feedback

tier_3_clarification_and_conflict_resolu.description: Meta-questions invoked dynamically when the SLM detects inconsistencies between answers within or across capabilities. These questions resolve dependency violations, dimension contradictions, and logical impossibilities before the system commits to a maturity level assignment. If the user cannot resolve the inconsistency, the system conservatively infers the lower maturity level.

conflict_patterns.trigger: PRA-CS-GOV-03 assessed at Level 3+ but PRA-CS-GOV-01 assessed at Level 0-1
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You indicated that your organization defines mandatory security practices for the SDLC, but no formal security policy seems to exist. Could you clarify whether the security strategy is built on implicit working agreements rather than a documented policy?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If unanswered, lower PRA-CS-GOV-03 to match PRA-CS-GOV-01 level.

conflict_patterns.trigger: PRA-CS-GOV-06 assessed at Level 3+ but PRA-CS-GOV-03 assessed at Level 0-1
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You mentioned an organizational metrics catalog, but no security strategy appears to be defined. Are the metrics anchored to an implicit strategy, or are they collected without strategic alignment?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If unanswered, lower PRA-CS-GOV-06 to Level 2 (team-defined metrics without strategic grounding).

conflict_patterns.trigger: PRA-CS-RISK-02 assessed at Level 3+ but PRA-CS-RISK-01 assessed at Level 0-1
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You mentioned a formal security write-off for releasing software, but no risk evaluation framework appears to exist. Is the write-off based on a formal risk assessment process, or on subjective judgment?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If unanswered, lower PRA-CS-RISK-02 to Level 1.

conflict_patterns.trigger: PRA-CS-STEST-02 assessed at Level 2+ but PRA-CS-STEST-01 assessed at Level 0-1
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You indicated that security tests are automated in the pipeline, but no security testing strategy seems to be defined. Are the automated tests part of an implicit strategy, or were they added opportunistically?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If unanswered, lower PRA-CS-STEST-02 to Level 1.

conflict_patterns.trigger: PRA-CS-OPSEC-05 assessed at Level 2+ but PRA-CS-OPSEC-04 assessed at Level 0
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You indicated that security issues are detected and treated through a defined process, but no monitoring capability exists. How are security problems being detected without monitoring?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If unanswered, lower PRA-CS-OPSEC-05 to Level 0.

conflict_patterns.trigger: PRA-CS-OPSEC-07 assessed at Level 2+ but PRA-CS-OPSEC-06 assessed at Level 0-1
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You described a team-defined response process, but no dedicated response team or trained individuals appear to exist. Are response procedures followed by general development staff, or is there informal role assignment?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If unanswered, lower PRA-CS-OPSEC-07 to Level 1 (ad-hoc process).

conflict_patterns.trigger: PRA-CS-TECH-01 assessed at Level 2+ but PRA-CS-REQ-02 assessed at Level 0-1
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You mentioned that your teams have internal definitions for implementing security requirements, but no classification of security features appears to exist. Are teams defining their own security features independently?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If confirmed (teams define independently), accept Level 2 for PRA-CS-TECH-01 but flag lack of consistency. If unanswered, lower to Level 1.

conflict_patterns.trigger: PRA-CS-CFGM-04 assessed at Level 2+ but PRA-CS-CFGM-01 assessed at Level 0
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You indicated that an SBOM is produced automatically, but no software asset inventory exists. Is the SBOM generated from build tooling without an underlying asset catalog?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If confirmed (tooling-based SBOM), accept Level 2 but note the gap. If unanswered, lower PRA-CS-CFGM-04 to Level 1.

conflict_patterns.trigger: OCM-05 = 'Undefined' but any practice assessed at Level 3+ with Scope = Organizational
conflict_patterns.dependency: Cross-cutting: SDLC formality bounds organizational scope
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You described your SDLC as undefined, but some practices appear to operate at an organizational level. Are there informal organizational agreements that serve as a de facto SDLC definition?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If confirmed, upgrade OCM-05 to Basic. If unanswered, cap all practices at Level 2 (Team scope maximum).

conflict_patterns.trigger: OCM-10 = 'Minimal awareness' but any practice assessed at Level 3+ with Knowledge = Proficient or Mastered
conflict_patterns.dependency: Cross-cutting: Knowledge baseline bounds knowledge dimension
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You rated your teams' cybersecurity knowledge as minimal, but some practices appear to operate at a proficient or mastered level. Is there a subset of specialized team members driving these practices?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If confirmed (specialized subset), accept practice levels but flag Knowledge dimension inconsistency for improvement planning. If unanswered, lower to Level 1.

conflict_patterns.trigger: PRA-CS-CI-01 assessed at Level 3+ but PRA-CS-CI-02 assessed at Level 0-1
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You indicated organizational CI capability with quality control gates, but the build process appears to be manual or not defined. Is the CI orchestrating a process that is automated at the tooling level but not formally documented?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If unanswered, lower PRA-CS-CI-01 to Level 2.

conflict_patterns.trigger: PRA-CS-RISK-05 assessed at Level 2+ but PRA-CS-RISK-04 assessed at Level 0-1
conflict_patterns.clarification_question: You described per-project threat assessments, but the organization does not appear to systematically identify vulnerabilities or attackers. Are threat assessments being performed without a prior vulnerability identification process?
conflict_patterns.fallback_rule: If unanswered, lower PRA-CS-RISK-05 to Level 1.
